course.title: EV Awareness & Safety
course.subtitle: Master the fundamentals of electric vehicle technology, identification, safety protocols, and charging — keeping you and your colleagues safe.

components.body: Your essential guide to identifying, understanding, and working safely around electric and hybrid vehicles.
components.imagePrompt: Sleek modern electric vehicle in a professional workshop environment, dramatic lighting with cyan and lime accent glows, dark moody atmosphere, high-end automotive photography style, 1920x1080

_graphic.alt: Modern electric vehicle in a professional workshop with dramatic lighting

components.body:
This course is designed to improve your knowledge of Electric Vehicles (EVs) and make you aware of the associated risks, so that you may exercise appropriate care for yourself and others when working near an Electric Vehicle.

Important: This first-level course does not qualify you to work directly on high voltage systems. You should inform your colleagues of your current trained level and request further assistance from a suitably trained person when necessary.
By the end of this course, you will be able to:

_items.title: Know about the types of electric and hybrid vehicles available
State how to identify electric/hybrid vehicles and give examples of those currently available
Understand the hazards around motor vehicle high energy electrical systems
State safety precautions to be taken before approaching and working around electric/hybrid vehicles
State how to identify motor vehicle high energy cabling and associated components
State how the vehicle may be safely charged using an external source

components.body: Please enter your details before continuing.
Choose the role that best describes your work. This helps us tailor the course content to your needs.

_items.title: Non-Technical
_items.body: Site logistics, car parking and movements, valeting.

_items.title: Semi-Technical
_items.body: Service advisor, customer liaison.

_items.title: Technical
_items.body: Bodyshop and workshop technicians, workshop manager.

components.body:
It is important that you can recognise an electric vehicle when it comes into your workplace. You will then be ready to exercise the correct procedures necessary for a safe visit.

Identifying features of electric vehicles differ between manufacturers. You may need to exercise your skill and experience to make sure you know what type of vehicle you are looking at — getting it wrong could be risky.
components.imagePrompt: Close-up of an electric vehicle badge and charge port on a modern car, professional automotive photography, dark background with cyan accent lighting, 800x600

_graphic.alt: Close-up of an electric vehicle badge and charging port

components.instruction: Select each panel to learn about the key visual identifiers.

_items.title: Number Plate
_items.body: Fully electric vehicles may have a green border on their number plate, though this is not mandatory.

_items.title: Badging
_items.body: Many EVs will have a badge indicating that they are an electric vehicle. Look for manufacturer-specific EV emblems.

_items.title: Charge Port
_items.body: On Battery Electric Vehicles (BEV) and Plug-in Hybrids (PHEV) there will be a battery charge lead connector port somewhere on the vehicle.

_items.title: Orange High Voltage Cables
_items.body: Bright-coloured (typically orange) high voltage cables are a clear indicator of an electric or hybrid vehicle.

_items.title: Warning Labels
_items.body: Look for high voltage warning labels, particularly in the engine bay and near battery components.

_items.title: Instrument Cluster
_items.body: EV-specific warnings, messages, and power flow/energy use screens on the dashboard indicate an electric drivetrain.

components.body:
You can also identify an EV by key features of the interior. Look for the power flow/energy use screen, absence of a traditional gear selector, and the instrument cluster showing battery charge level rather than a fuel gauge.

The power controller/inverter and high voltage warning labels under the bonnet are additional confirmation.
components.imagePrompt: Modern electric vehicle interior dashboard showing digital displays and energy flow screen, clean professional photography, dark theme with cool blue accent lighting, 800x600

_graphic.alt: Electric vehicle interior dashboard with digital energy display

components.displayTitle: EV Identification Features by Vehicle Type
components.body: Not all types of EV share the same identifying features. Use this reference table to understand which identifiers apply to each vehicle type.
Understanding the differences between vehicle types is essential for safe working practices.
components.imagePrompt: Dramatic wide shot of multiple electric vehicles lined up in a modern showroom, dark moody atmosphere with cyan and green accent lighting, cinematic composition, 1920x800

_graphic.alt: Multiple electric vehicles in a modern showroom

components.instruction: Select each tab to explore the different types of electric and hybrid vehicles.

_items.title: ICE
_items.body:
Internal Combustion Engine

Powered solely by a petrol or diesel engine — no electric motor or battery for propulsion. Energy is produced by burning fuel and transmitted mechanically to the wheels. Emits CO₂ and other pollutants. Still common globally, but being phased out in many regions due to environmental regulations.

_items.title: BEV
_items.body:
Battery Electric Vehicle

Driven entirely by an electric motor, with no internal combustion engine. Has a large capacity high voltage battery to provide the required driving range. Instant torque gives strong performance with no engine noise. The high voltage system can be identified by orange or brightly coloured cabling.

_items.title: HEV
_items.body:
Hybrid Electric Vehicle

Usually fitted with an ICE, high voltage battery, and electric motor assembly. Some use their electric motor simultaneously with the ICE to drive the wheels (Parallel Hybrid). Others use the petrol engine to charge the battery only (Series Hybrid). Some can switch between both modes (Series/Parallel Hybrid).

_items.title: PHEV
_items.body:
Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle

The high voltage battery can be charged using an external mains charger. Has a larger capacity battery than standard hybrids, increasing electric-only range. The vehicle also has a combustion engine for extended range. Can operate as Parallel or Series hybrid depending on the model.

_items.title: MHEV
_items.body:
Mild Hybrid Electric Vehicle

Uses a combustion engine with a belt-integrated starter generator that provides extra power when pulling away or accelerating. Cannot operate in fully electric mode. Typically operates at 48V — below the recognised definition of "High Voltage". The 48V system wiring is often coloured purple or blue.

components.body:
Electric vehicles form a family tree of related technologies. At the top level, vehicles split into ICE, Hybrid, Battery Electric, and Fuel Cell Electric categories.

Hybrids further divide into Mild Hybrid, Parallel Hybrid, Series Hybrid, Series/Parallel Hybrid, and Plug-in Hybrid variants. Each has different safety implications for workshop staff.
components.imagePrompt: Technical illustration showing electric vehicle powertrain family tree diagram, clean modern infographic style with dark background and cyan/lime accent colors, 800x600

_graphic.alt: EV family tree showing the different categories of electric vehicles

components.body: Compare the key benefits and drawbacks of the main EV types.
In the context of Electric Vehicles, any voltage greater than 60 volts is classified as a high voltage system.

components.instruction: Navigate through the key components found in electric vehicles.

_items.title: HV Battery Pack
_items.body: High voltage traction battery — typically 60–800V. Powers the electric drive motors. This is the primary energy storage component and represents the most significant safety consideration.

_items.title: Power Electronics Controller
_items.body: Contains the inverter to change AC to DC current and vice versa, plus the electronic controller that manages power flow throughout the vehicle.

_items.title: Electric Drive Motor
_items.body: Powered by high voltage AC from the electronics controller to drive the vehicle. In some configurations, it can also work as a generator during regenerative braking.

_items.title: DC/DC Converter
_items.body: Converts high voltage from the traction battery to low voltage (12V) for the vehicle's conventional electrical systems — lights, radio, and control modules.

_items.title: Battery Cooling System
_items.body: Maintains optimal battery temperature. Essential for performance, longevity, and safety. Overheating batteries can lead to thermal runaway.

_items.title: On-Board Charger
_items.body: Found in BEVs and PHEVs. Takes mains electricity and converts it to charge the vehicle's high voltage battery. Connected via the mains charging port.

On-board charger unit connected to EV charging port, technical photography, dark moody atmosphere, 800x600

components.body:
You can verify whether a vehicle is electric by using the UK Government's vehicle enquiry service at vehicleenquiry.service.gov.uk.

Enter the vehicle registration number to see the vehicle's fuel type, CO₂ emissions, and other details that will confirm whether it's an EV, hybrid, or conventional vehicle.
components.imagePrompt: Screenshot-style illustration of a government vehicle database interface showing electric vehicle details, clean UI design, dark theme, 800x600

_graphic.alt: Government vehicle enquiry service showing EV details

_items.label: Maximum battery voltage in modern EVs

components.body: Which of the following is the best way to confirm whether a vehicle entering your workshop is electric?

_items.text: Check for a green number plate band
Look for EV badging on the vehicle
Check the vehicle registration on the government enquiry service and look for multiple identification features
Listen for engine noise

_feedback.correct: Correct! Using multiple identification methods, including the government database, is the most reliable approach since not all EVs share the same visual identifiers.

_incorrect.final: Not quite. While individual features can indicate an EV, using multiple identification methods including the government vehicle enquiry service is the most reliable approach.

components.body: A Mild Hybrid Electric Vehicle (MHEV) typically operates at what voltage?

_feedback.correct: Correct! MHEVs typically operate at 48V, which is below the recognised definition of "High Voltage" for electric vehicles.

_incorrect.final: Incorrect. Mild Hybrid Electric Vehicles typically operate at 48V — below the 60V high voltage threshold.

components.body:
The UK Health and Safety Executive warns that voltages present in electric and hybrid vehicles are significantly higher than conventional vehicles — currently up to 650V DC compared to 12/24V DC in traditional vehicles.

In dry conditions, accidental contact with parts that are live at voltages above 110V DC can be fatal.

Battery systems may contain chemicals that can be harmful if released. They also store significant amounts of energy that can give rise to explosion if not dealt with correctly.
EVs introduce hazards into the workplace in addition to those normally associated with vehicle repair and maintenance.

_items.title: Electric Shock
_items.body: High voltage components and cabling capable of delivering a fatal electric shock. Stored electrical energy can cause arc/flash.

_items.title: Silent Operation
_items.body: People may be unaware of vehicles moving as they are silent when electrically driven. AVAS warning systems may not sound as expected.

_items.title: Battery Hazards
_items.body: Potential release of explosive gases and harmful liquids if batteries are damaged or incorrectly modified.

_items.title: Residual Voltage
_items.body: Components may retain dangerous voltage even when the vehicle is switched off.

_items.title: Unexpected Movement
_items.body: Electric motors or the vehicle itself may move unexpectedly due to magnetic forces within the motors.

_items.title: Medical Devices
_items.body: The electrical systems on the vehicle may affect medical devices such as pacemakers.

imagePrompts.prompt: High voltage warning sign on EV component, dramatic lighting with orange glow, dark industrial background, 600x400
Silent electric vehicle moving through workshop, motion blur effect, dark atmosphere, 600x400

components.displayTitle: ECE R100 — What Counts as High Voltage?
components.body:
ECE R100 defines "High Voltage" as an electric component or circuit with working voltage between >60V and ≤1500V DC or >30V and ≤1000V AC (root mean square).

These values are used specifically when referring to Electric Vehicles. Please note that high voltage is defined differently in other industry sectors.
components.imagePrompt: High voltage warning symbol with electrical safety equipment including insulated gloves and tools, dark professional photography with yellow/orange warning color accents, 800x600

_graphic.alt: High voltage warning symbol with safety equipment

_items.definition: Valeting, sales, and other lower risk activities
Incident response including emergency services and vehicle recovery
Maintenance and repair excluding high voltage electrical systems
Working on high voltage electrical systems

components.instruction: Click each card to reveal the effect of electric shock on the body.

_items.back: A voltage as low as 50V can block electrical signals between the brain and muscles, potentially stopping the heart from beating properly.
Electric shock can prevent the person from breathing by disrupting the nerve signals that control respiratory muscles.
Electrical current passing through the body causes severe muscle spasms that can prevent the victim from releasing the source.
Burns at entry and exit points, fall injuries from being thrown or losing balance, and internal tissue damage are common secondary effects.

components.body: If you encounter someone receiving an electric shock, follow these steps in order.

_items.title: Isolate the Power
_items.body: For mains systems (vehicle chargers for BEV/PHEV), turn off the power at the isolation switch. For high voltage vehicles, the supply is normally disconnected when turning the vehicle off — but consider there may be a fault.

_items.title: Do Not Touch the Casualty
_items.body: Do not touch the casualty if they are still in contact with the electrical source — you are at risk of electrocution.

_items.title: Use a Rescue Hook
_items.body: If safe to do so, use a purpose-designed insulated rescue hook to separate them from the power supply. The rescue hook should be kept visible and accessible at the EV charging bay at all times.

_items.title: Call 999
_items.body: Call emergency services immediately for professional medical assistance.

_items.title: Perform Primary Survey
_items.body: Once contact is broken, perform a primary survey and treat injuries. Seek assistance from the company first aid person if available.

_items.title: Secure the Area
_items.body: Cordon off the vehicle using a non-conductive safety barrier chain. No one should work on the vehicle until it has been made safe and the risk reassessed.

components.body: For valeting, sales, and lower risk activities around EVs:

_items.title: Keep remote operation keys away from vehicles to prevent accidental power-up
Be aware that EVs may not make normal engine sounds when moving
Note that AVAS (Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System) sounds may not be what you expect
Alert pedestrians and co-workers when driving EVs near people
Avoid pressure washing under-body areas — high voltage cables (usually orange) can be damaged
Refer to manufacturer guidance before valeting engine bay areas

components.body: To work safely around EVs, specialist equipment is required. Some of this equipment you may already see in your day-to-day work. Understanding the purpose of each item is critical for workplace safety.
What is the primary purpose of the vehicle identification sign placed on an electric vehicle in a workshop?

_items.text: Tells staff that the vehicle might need charging
Should be present on every electric vehicle entering a workshop so that all staff are aware it is an EV
Allows the service advisor to find their cars easily

_feedback.correct: Correct! The sign ensures that all workshop staff are immediately aware they are dealing with an electric vehicle.

_incorrect.final: Incorrect. The primary purpose is to ensure all staff are aware the vehicle is an EV, so appropriate safety precautions are taken.

components.body: What is the primary purpose of the barrier chain/cordon used around electric vehicles?

_items.text: Used to hang signs on the vehicle
Stops anyone moving the vehicle from the workbay
Alerts others and prevents unauthorised access

_feedback.correct: Correct! The barrier chain alerts others to the presence of an EV and prevents unauthorised access to the work area.

_incorrect.final: Incorrect. The primary purpose is to alert others and prevent unauthorised access to the vehicle work area.

components.body: What is the primary purpose of the key signal blocker pouch?

_items.text: Prevents the key from being stolen
Prevents accidental power up on keyless start vehicles by blocking the key receiving and sending signals to the vehicle
Stops the key being lost

_feedback.correct: Correct! The signal blocker prevents the vehicle from accidentally powering up when the keyless key is nearby.

_incorrect.final: Incorrect. The primary purpose is to block signals between the key and vehicle, preventing accidental power-up.

components.body: What is the primary purpose of the insulated rescue hook?

_items.text: To be used by suitably trained staff in an emergency to remove a casualty from the source of an electric shock
To be used by the workshop manager to drag a technician away from an electric shock source
To move the high voltage cables away from the car if they become short circuited

_feedback.correct: Correct! The rescue hook is specifically designed for trained staff to safely separate a casualty from an electrical source.

_incorrect.final: Incorrect. The rescue hook is for suitably trained staff to use in an emergency to safely remove a casualty from an electrical source.

components.body: Understanding charging terminology and systems is essential for safe EV handling.
components.imagePrompt: Modern EV charging station at night with multiple charging points, dramatic lighting with glowing cyan and green LED indicators, cinematic dark atmosphere, 1920x800

_graphic.alt: Modern EV charging station at night

_items.definition: The term used to describe electrical pressure. ICE vehicles use 12V, trucks 24V, mild hybrids 48V, hybrids 200–400V typically, and full EVs 350–800V typically.
The measurement used to work out electrical flow. A higher amp reading indicates a larger current is flowing. There is a direct relationship between voltage, amps, and circuit resistance.
Refers to energy consumption. Home charging typically delivers 2.3kW. Wall boxes deliver 7.4–22kW. Rapid chargers are 50kW+ with the fastest in the UK reaching 360kW.

_nodes.title: Home Socket
_nodes.body: 2.3kW — approximately 22.6 hours for a full charge of a 52kWh battery

_nodes.title: Wall Box
_nodes.body: 7.4–22kW — significantly faster than a home socket, suitable for overnight charging

_nodes.title: Rapid Charger
_nodes.body: 50kW+ — fastest option, can charge to 80% in under an hour depending on the vehicle

_nodes.title: Ultra-Rapid
_nodes.body: Up to 360kW — the fastest chargers currently available in the UK, though vehicles may not yet accept this rate

components.body: You've covered all the essential knowledge for EV awareness and safety. Complete the final assessment below.
components.imagePrompt: Professional workshop team standing near an electric vehicle with safety equipment visible, confident and professional atmosphere, dark moody lighting with cyan accents, 1920x1080

_graphic.alt: Professional workshop team with EV safety equipment

components.body: What is the primary purpose of insulated gloves (Class 0 rated 1500V DC) in an EV workshop?

_items.text: Keeps hands clean and dry
Prevents the spread of infection
Should be worn by suitably trained persons when working on the high voltage system of the vehicle

_feedback.correct: Correct! Insulated gloves rated to 1500V DC are essential PPE for anyone working directly on high voltage systems.

_incorrect.final: Incorrect. These specialised gloves must be worn by trained personnel when working on high voltage vehicle systems for protection against electric shock.

components.body: What is the primary purpose of warning signs placed around a vehicle undergoing high voltage work?

_items.text: Alerts others to the risks that are present
Stops anyone moving the vehicle from the workbay
Prevents access to the workbay

_feedback.correct: Correct! Warning signs alert all staff and visitors to the specific risks present during high voltage work.

_incorrect.final: Incorrect. The primary purpose of warning signs is to alert others to the risks that are present.

components.body: What is the primary purpose of vehicle status signs?

_items.text: Tells staff that the vehicle might need charging
Placed on the vehicle to let everyone know what the condition and operational status is
Allows the service advisor to find their cars easily

_feedback.correct: Correct! Vehicle status signs communicate the current condition and operational status to all staff.

_incorrect.final: Incorrect. Vehicle status signs are placed on vehicles to communicate their current condition and operational status to all staff.

components.body: What is the primary purpose of the "Do Not Start / Work in Progress" sign?

_items.text: Should be used to prevent damage or danger being caused by unauthorised starting during the repair process
Should be used to save fuel by asking people not to start the vehicle
Should be used to keep the steering wheel clean

_feedback.correct: Correct! This sign prevents unauthorised starting of the vehicle during repairs, which could endanger technicians.

_incorrect.final: Incorrect. The sign's primary purpose is to prevent damage or danger from unauthorised starting during the repair process.
